en.extract:
The Pemba scops owl (Otus pembaensis) is a species of owl in the family Strigidae. It is endemic to Pemba Island which is part of and off the coast of Tanzania.


Taxonomy
The Pemba scops owl was formerly regarded as a subspecies of the Madagascar scops owl, Otus rutilus sensu lato, due to morphological similarities but genetic research has shown that it is closer to the clade in which the African scops owl, Otus senegalensis, is situated.


Description
The Pemba scops owl is a medium-sized scops-owl with short ear-tufts. There are two colour morphs, a brown morph which is mainly pale rufous-brown with light streaking on the head and faint barring on paler underparts and a rufous morph which is a bright, rich rufous, that is paler on the underwing coverts. Both morphs show a pale scapular band, whitish in the brown morph and pale rufous in the rufous morph. The bill is black while the cere is greenish-yellow and the eyes and legs are yellow. It is 15 cm (5.9 in) tall with a 45 cm (18 in) wingspan.


Voice
The call is a single "hoo" note which is made at irregular intervals or in a vast rapid series of 4–6 notes given at intervals of half a second. The pair duets with the male calls being shorter, and lower in pitch.


Distribution and habitat
The Pemba scops owl is endemic to Pemba, the northern island of the Zanzibar archipelago, part of Tanzania, off the coast of east Africa. On Pemba this owl is found in all wooded habitats from native forest to overgrown plantations of cloves and mango. However, it is most common in native forest.


Behaviour
The biology of the Pemba scops owl is little known. It is nocturnal and roosts among foliage or in dense undergrowth during the day. It starts calling soon after sunset and then hunts. Its diet is mainly insects which may be caught in flight, gleaned from leaves or caught on the ground after a short glide from a perch. The breeding behaviour is almost unknown, although it possibly breeds from August to October and nests in natural holes in trees.


Conservation status
The Pemba scops owl is listed as a Vulnerable by the IUCN because it is restricted to Pemba, the population continues to decline and there is a loss of habitat as local farmers clear plantations to make way for open agricultural fields. It is now largely confined to the two small remaining native forests: Ngezi, which is 14 km2 (5.4 sq mi) in area, and Msitu Mkuu, at only 3 km2 (1.2 sq mi). The population is estimated to be between 1,500 and 4,500 breeding pairs, although it is in rapid decline.


References


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet.

The '''Pemba scops owl''' ('''''Otus pembaensis''''') is a species of [[owl]] in the family [[Strigidae]]. It is [[endemism|endemic]] to [[Pemba Island]] which is part of and off the coast of [[Tanzania]].

==Taxonomy==
The Pemba scops owl was formerly regarded as a [[subspecies]] of the [[Rainforest scops owl|Madagascar scops owl]], ''Otus rutilus'' ''sensu lato'', due to morphological similarities but genetic research has shown that it is closer to the [[clade]] in which the [[African scops owl]], ''Otus senegalensis'', is situated.<ref name = Fuchs>{{cite journal | last1 = Fuchs| first1= Jérôme|display-authors=etal| year = 2008 | title = Tracing the colonization history of the Indian Ocean scops-owls (Strigiformes: Otus) with further insight into the spatio-temporal origin of the Malagasy avifauna | doi = 10.1186/1471-2148-8-197 | journal = BMC Evolutionary Biology | volume=8 | page=197| pmid= 18611281| pmc = 2483963 | doi-access= free}}</ref>

==Description==
The Pemba scops owl is a medium-sized scops-owl with short ear-tufts. There are two colour morphs, a brown morph which is mainly pale rufous-brown with light streaking on the head and faint barring on paler underparts and a rufous morph which is a bright, rich rufous, that is paler on the underwing coverts. Both morphs show a pale scapular band, whitish in the brown morph and pale rufous in the rufous morph. The bill is black while the cere is greenish-yellow and the eyes and legs are yellow. It is {{convert|15|cm|in|abbr=on}} tall with a {{convert|45|cm|in|abbr=on}} wingspan.<ref name = Kemp>{{cite book | last1 = Kemp | first1 = Alan | last2 = Kemp |first2 = Meg | year = 1998 |  title = Birds of Prey of Africa and its Islands | pages = 318–319 | publisher = New Holland | isbn =1-85974-100-2}}</ref>

===Voice===
The call is a single "hoo" note which is made at irregular intervals or in a vast rapid series of 4–6 notes given at intervals of half a second.<ref name = Kemp/> The pair duets with the male calls being shorter, and lower in pitch.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

==Distribution and habitat==
The Pemba scops owl is endemic to Pemba, the northern island of the [[Zanzibar]] archipelago, part of Tanzania, off the coast of east Africa. On Pemba this owl is found in all wooded habitats from native forest to overgrown plantations of [[cloves]] and [[mango]].<ref name=Arkive>{{cite web | url = http://www.arkive.org/pemba-scops-owl/otus-pembaensis/ | title = Pemba scops-owl (''Otus pembaensis'') | access-date = 27 October 2016 | publisher = Wildscreen | work = Wildscreen Arkive | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20161027194938/http://www.arkive.org/pemba-scops-owl/otus-pembaensis/ | archive-date = 2016-10-27 }}</ref> However, it is most common in native forest.<ref name = planetofbirds>{{cite web | url = http://www.planetofbirds.com/strigiformes-strigidae-pemba-scops-owl-otus-pembaensis | title = Pemba Scops Owl (Otus pembaensis) | access-date = 27 October 2016 | publisher = Planet of Birds}}</ref>

==Behaviour==
The biology of the Pemba scops owl is little known. It is nocturnal and roosts among foliage or in dense undergrowth during the day. It starts calling soon after sunset and then hunts. Its diet is mainly insects which may be caught in flight, gleaned from leaves or caught on the ground after a short glide from a perch. The breeding behaviour is almost unknown, although it possibly breeds from August to October and nests in natural holes in trees.<ref name = Arkive/>

==Conservation status==
The Pemba scops owl is listed as a [[Vulnerable species|Vulnerable]] by the [[IUCN]] because it is restricted to Pemba, the population continues to decline and there is a loss of habitat as local farmers clear plantations to make way for open agricultural fields. It is now largely confined to the two small remaining native forests: Ngezi, which is {{convert|14|km2|abbr=on}} in area, and Msitu Mkuu, at only {{convert|3|km2|abbr=on}}. The population is estimated to be between 1,500 and 4,500 breeding pairs, although it is in rapid decline.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />
